narration: You walked in for a $160 blender, but they put a $400 one right next to it. Suddenly $160 feels like a steal — that's not luck. It's called anchoring.
visual: Two price tags spring in side by side — red $400 labeled ANCHOR, amber $160 labeled FEELS CHEAP. Person silhouette appears reaching for the cheaper one.

narration: Anchoring was discovered in 1974 by Kahneman and Tversky. Your brain locks onto the first number it sees and adjusts everything else from there.
visual: Three steps slide in sequentially: step 1 YOU SEE $400 FIRST in red, step 2 YOU SEE $160 NEXT in amber, step 3 $160 FEELS CHEAP in green. Badge: DISCOVERED 1974.

narration: Restaurant menus do this on purpose. That $95 Wagyu steak isn't meant to sell — it's the anchor. Next to it, your $42 salmon suddenly feels responsible.
visual: SVG restaurant menu card — $95 Wagyu Ribeye at top in red, $42 Atlantic Salmon highlighted in amber as THE REAL TARGET badge.

narration: Car dealers always walk you to the $58,000 loaded model first. By the time you see the $43,000 base, your brain screams savings. It's not.
visual: SVG car with two floating price badges — red $58,000 FULLY LOADED, amber $43,000 BASE MODEL. Text: YOUR BRAIN says I'M SAVING $15K vs REALITY: SPENDING $43K.

narration: Anchored shoppers spend 28% more on average. On a $50,000 annual spend, that's $14,000 leaving your wallet every year because of a number they chose — not you.
visual: Piggy bank SVG springs in. Counter animates from $0 to $14,000. Progress bar fills to 28% labeled 28% OVERSPEND FROM ANCHORING.

narration: The fix? Research your target price before you walk in. Come with your own anchor. Don't let the store set it — and you could keep up to $14,000 a year.
visual: Three checkmarks slide in: RESEARCH PRICE FIRST, SET YOUR OWN ANCHOR, IGNORE THE DECOY. Amber badge pulses: KEEP UP TO $14,000 PER YEAR.
